posts.name: Fred C
posts.cooked:
So I’m at a restaurant in Miami sipping on a $50 6oz pour of 2014 Domaine Leflaive Bourgogne Blanc. Very ripe. Drinking fine now but lacking acidity and Leflaive matchstick. This will probably not age well and will probably premox.

Then I realized that maybe there really isn’t any motivation for producers to “solve” the premox problem.

If they can sell it to restaurants who can mark it up 4-5x who sell a huge volume nightly why would they want to go back to a product that takes 10-20 years to come around?

If producers wanted to, they could just emulate what Coche and PYCM does or in the case of Leflaive, just go back to what they were doing.

So maybe the premox “problem” isn’t really a problem at all for 80% of the market. Just us wine geeks who want to age our wines for decades to develops nuances.

At the end of the day, money talks and maybe premox isn’t really that big of a “problem” for producers.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
larry schaffer:

What do you think the percentages of people who hold these to age them for decades are these days? My guess is that as the OP suggests, it probably is not that high . . .

I would guess very low, though the percentage would probably be higher for the most expensive producers and vineyards.

I’ve been saying the same thing as the OP - I don’t know that Burgundy is feeling much pressure to diagnose and solve the problem. Sales and prices seem to be going strong. If they are feeling any pressure, it might be at the very expensive level (e.g. Domaine Leflaive), where more buyers age the wines and are knowledgeable enough to know and to care about premox.

And then you consider how difficult it would be to diagnose, since (1) most producers are small so neither incentivized nor having the resources to do a lot of investigation and experimentation, and (2) you can’t tell whether something you tried worked or not unless you (a) have a good baseline for premox rates in the past and (b) wait 7-10 years to see how the change worked out or didn’t.

Lastly, Burgundy is being made in a riper and lower acid style in recent years, which is drinkable early and probably more popular with consumers, and I suspect that is the reason, or a big part of the reason, the wines aren’t as well protected against oxidation. If that’s true, they may very well not even want to solve premox, since it might mean making wines that aren’t as popular with consumers (most of whom aren’t going to age the wines for long enough for them to get premoxed).

And there is an obvious solution - use screwcaps. Even if closures were not the reason for premox (I doubt they are, myself), a screwcap could still be the solution. But of course the romance and the tradition and it’s not proven blah blah blah.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
Most producers I’ve spoken with are aware, concerned and would like to do something about the issue. But few are certain about what to do, and for good reason. It just seems very unlikely that any single producer could possibly do careful enough testing to know for certain the causes and remedies of premox. They can guess and approximate, which is what they do. But is it sulfur additions, number of punch-downs, acidity, global warming, riper picking, etc. etc.?

I think it would take a well-funded independent body (perhaps the French government) to do the long-term controlled testing to determine the best practices to minimize premox while making the kind of wine producers want to make. Is this happening? I’d bet that the French government has higher priorities for their wine/agriculture bureaucracy budget.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I think this is a very astute observation. There has been amazingly little consumer push-back in response to premox, and white Burgundy prices have continued to rise, in some cases asymptotically. When old customers stopped buying their e.g. Ramonet allocations, new consumers who were not in the habit of forgetting the wines in the cellar for a decade plus stepped up to take the wines.

Now we see merchants and producers trying to revise consumer expectations: “if a village Meursault is oxidized after ten years it isn’t prematurely oxidized” etc. With pre-premox bottles in circulation that’s still easy to refute, but in another decade or so, with generational change in the wine world, white Burgundy’s true capacity for greatness with bottle age may be largely forgotten. It’s a depressing prospect.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
As far as assigning fault, I think the Lavigne-Dubourdieu study that assigns blame to the lack of lees/glutathione in the wine due to faulty use of the new presses was pretty much right on.
Wineries in the Loire and in Burgundy bought these presses beginning around 20 years ago.

When I started in wine, back in the early '70s, older wine and having a cellar in which to turn young wine into old were both prized. Now, selling older wine seems a struggle. To most people, the idea of older white wine seems bizarre. Even red wine out of step with other releases can seem weird. If everyone else has released their 2015 Pinot Noir, then your 2013 must have something wrong with it.


People in Burgundy are concerned. SO2 levels are up; some are going back to older presses. I think the problem was caused by a perfect storm:
1/using less SO2
2/riper musts
3/new presses…less glutathione in the wine
4/a market that called for restaurant ready white wines

One grower said to us, Most of my wine is sold and consumed within two years of bottling, but then ten years later somebody drinks the wine and judges it.

Andy points out that wines from most regions are aging as they were 25 years ago. Nobody talks about premox with California Chardonnay. The wines that aged well then age well now. The wines that fell part five years on still fall part. We all face the same global warming issues and use the same closures.

I have discussed premox with many Burgundian winemakers…usually the wines who make red wine!

andy velebil:

A friend of mine, a researcher in Bordeaux, gave a talk on this subject in Burgundy a few years back. He got two hours worth of questions.

On premox itself, or motivation to change? For the former, I don’t think many questions can be answered with authority by anyone unless they are about theories rather than facts. Almost nothing is known for sure in terms of cause.

Since no other region has the issues Burgundy does, we can rule out corks, global warming, and a host of other things except winemaking, right?

Well, yes and no. Lots of other places have never made Chardonnay that ages for 10-30 years, including most of California. Exceptions to this exist but not on the scale of age worthy white Burgundy.
That said, the fact that white Burgundy tastes far more ripe, rich, and textured on release these days could be a sign that as Burgundians are/have been influenced by other areas where the winemaking style simply isn’t what is required for long term aging. Most likely because the process for long term aging is counter productive to sales to a new and expanding audience.
Also, climate is probably a part of pre-mox. Chablis used to lose 2-3 vintages per decade to weather, now it never happens. Côte d’Or whites are higher abv than they used to be, and higher pH, which has a direct effect on the efficacy of SO2 additions.

posts.name: Chris de W.
posts.cooked:
andy velebil:
Doug Schulman:
Mel Knox:

A friend of mine, a researcher in Bordeaux, gave a talk on this subject in Burgundy a few years back. He got two hours worth of questions.

On premox itself, or motivation to change? For the former, I don’t think many questions can be answered with authority by anyone unless they are about theories rather than facts. Almost nothing is known for sure in terms of cause.

Since no other region has the issues Burgundy does, we can rule out corks, global warming, and a host of other things except winemaking, right?

That’s not correct IMO. I’ve had many premoxed whites from the Rhone, Loire and Bordeaux. People probably focus on Burgundy because that type of wine is relatively often stored for a long time compared to other dry white wines.

The most likely cause is variability in air permeability of corks. Using less sulphite or picking more ripe fruit can perhaps make the wines more vulnerable, but that cannot explain the random nature of the occurrence of premox (great variation in the level of oxidation of wines from the same vintage or even the same case of wine). It is well proven that the difference in air permeability between corks in the same batch of corks is huge and so far that is the only thing that I’ve read over the past years that could explain the random variability of premox.

Doug,

Have you read the Lavigne-Dubourdieu report??
It’s floating around here somewhere.

Do you mean this?

http://www.newbordeaux.com/dubourdieu-premox-in-whites

(Whoever designed that web page should have hired a professional. I had to highlight it to read it.)

Yes, I remember that being posted a while back. They have made a lot of assumptions and stated them as fact, saying things like “we have clearly shown the role of sotolon in the aroma of all premoxed white wines,” without citing any research or even clarifying the vagueness of the statement. Is sotolon playing a part in creating premox, or is it simply present when premox occurs? That word “role” seems to imply the former, yet they fail to show evidence of a connection in either way. They say limiting extraction of phenolic compounds during pressing is an important preventive measure, yet there is a very plausible, even likely, theory that says the opposite. If there is a winemaking connection at all, it should be noted that pressing has generally become more gentle, not more vigorous, around the beginning of the premox era. They say premox can occur in barrel, which shows an alarming lack of understanding of what we’re talking about when we discuss premox, considering they are highly educated in the field of wine chemistry and claim to have discovered a solution. They say reductive handling and frequent doses of SO2 are important when Clark Smith has found higher redox potential in wines that are not as reductively handled during elevage. I think there are also other problems that I don’t have time to get into now. What I see there are a lot of technical terms and facts being thrown around without much meaningful analysis, and their claim that they have found a solution, without any mention of long-term testing (which I have never heard of them doing) seems preposterous.

posts.name: Chris de W.
posts.cooked:
I think that the Lavigne-Dubourdieu report does not add any meaningful insight into the premix issue. They seem to state that higher levels of soloton are present in premoxed wines because soloton is formed as part of the oxidation process. Not really meaningful info.

Perhaps the most meaningful phrases are the following non-scientific words:

Of course, the defective ageing of white wines is most often discovered once the wine has been bottled. The most disconcerting aspect of premox in bottled wine is its very random nature. If a tasting jury is asked to evaluate the oxidative character of twelve bottles of the same dry white wine with a cork closure (same vintage, bottled the same day, with the same lot of corks, etc.), certain bottles may be found to be very aged, whereas others may show no sign of premature ageing whatsoever.
﻿
There is a very close correlation between the quantity of oxygen dissolved in the bottle and a wine’s propensity to suffer from premox. Furthermore, the more a wine’s aromas are judged to be oxidised by tasters, the more intense the wine’s orangey-yellow nuances (higher DO 420). Premoxed bottles have lower levels of free SO2. Naturally, the presence of sotolon is also closely correlated with the intensity of the oxidative character detected during tasting.
﻿
These results raise the very tricky issue of the best closure to use, which is too involved a subject to go into here. Significant variations in the dissolved oxygen level in wine have been observed only six months after bottling according to the type of closure used (natural cork, synthetic cork, technical corks, or screwcaps). The level of free SO2 is also very different depending on the type of closure. These results illustrate the extreme heterogeneity of permeability to gas of various closures.

They do not go any further into the subject of the closures which they do mention as a cause for variations in dissolved oxygen level in wine. Others have studied this and (also) found huge differences in air (and oxygen) entering the wine bottle depending on the type of closure used and discovered that the air permeability of corks varies tremendously.

That is a very easy explanation for random premox occurring, but it is also a very unwanted explanation for a big part of the wine world. Producers still have significant inventory of wine bottled with corks, wine producers and consumers are extremely conservative (on average) and do not like change, wine collectors do not want to hear that their huge collection of wine is sealed with a highly variable closure, and a significant part of the wine researchers are sponsored by big cork companies such as Amorim or other cork institutes.

All the talk about global warming, later harvesting, different presses, using less SO2 etcetera as possible causes is IMO distraction attention away from what is the likely cause for random premox. I really do not see how any of those factors is likely to cause one bottle of wine from the same batch/box to age much faster than another bottle.

Moreover, I do not believe that random premox is restricted to white wine. I believe that the cork variability is also a likely cause for the variation that one experiences in red wine after extended ageing (‘there are no great old wines … just great old (individual) bottles of wine’).

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
I have found Leflaive difficult to visit. A friend who imports their wine said two out of three visits get cancelled. Maybe this is not true any more. I do find that the reluctance of Burgundian winemakers to discuss this in the open does not allow for constructive suggestions.
I have been visiting Burgundy virtually very year since 1977. When I first visited, wineries were using those old-fashioned Vaslin presses in which the pommace gets squeezed between one or two plates.

In retrospect, the virtue of these presses was that the juice went into a drip pan and browned a bit. During fermentation those phenolics dropped out and that meant that the wine did not have to be protected from phenolic browning, unlike in California where unimaginable amounts of SO2 had been used.

Sometimes in the mid 90s, I startedto notice two trends:
1/bio dynamics…winemakers told me that could use less SO2 because the wine was bio…pass the crack pipe, homie…
2/new presses…state of the art membrane presses

The first time I encountered premox was at a gathering in Oregon in 2004 where two collectors opened up a passel of Montrachets…nearly all of them oxidized…little did I realize that this was the canary in the coal mine.

Then somebody started a 50 pages thread on the subject on Parker’s website and after about 15 pages I tossed in my two cents. I said that I thought the lower levels of SO2 in riper vintages were a culprit, along with the new presses…But why the new presses were to blame I could not explain. Was it a failure to let the juice brown??

The glutathione discussion opened a new vista for me on the subject. Winemakers I knew were using more SO2 and yet not getting anywhere.

Sometimes academic discussion of winemaking reminds me of what pitching coaches say to pitchers: throw strikes, but nothing too good…easy to say, hard to do.

The new presses offer the winemaker the possibility of less solids, phenolics etc. But there is no reason why they cannot be adjusted so the wine has plenty of lees in the barrel.

I can think of four wineries in California who use lots of lees contact and their whites age well: Au Bon Climat, the old Chalone, Ramey and Mt Eden.

I have always rejected the cork theory, as it implies that California winemakers get great corks and people in other markets are stupid and use crappy ones.

What has happened with the secret of producing great ageworthy white burgundy seems to have echoes of what happens when a golfer starts to doubt his driving technique, and the more he tries to fix it, the longer it takes to return to playing at the same level … if he ever does. Maybe Burgundy needs to find its own equivalent of Butch Harmon to fix premox…

John,
I do not think that is the key question.

People would be fine with white Burgundy if all the wines age as the best examples in a case do. The problem is mainly that many bottles out of a case/box of 12 are not living up to expectations.

Wines are perhaps now more vulnerable to oxidation for many reasons, such as global warming, other presses, plastic instead of lead capsules, less SO2, less filtering and riper harvesting. However, none of those reasons would explain the bottle variation.

Stated differently: if wine makers can fix the bottle variation and have most wines perform like the best bottles they currently produce, the problem would be solved. Adding a much higher dose of SO2 as I’m sure many growers are now doing as an easy measure will not take away the bottle variation. It could perhaps delay oxidation and ensure that more bottles are not overly oxidized before they are consumed but there will still be many disappointments if people trust the wines again and cellar them for longer times.

I’m not a wine grower, but if I’d be a wine grower and would want to solve this problem I would start with the most likely cause and change the wine closure. Not only is this by now a well proven solution to the problem, but it would also take away a part of my production process that causes huge variation in oxygen exposure to my wines once they are bottled. Taking that away would make it much easier to analyze the existence and effect of possible other causes.

I’ve had many oxidized white wines from the Loire and the Rhone valley. But how many cellar those wines and post on wine boards such as WineBerserkers? Probably very few compared to those cellaring and drinking white wines from Burgundy.

Check out the following article about Baumard, one of the ‘kings’ of the Loire who switched away from cork to solve premox:

Wine Spectator – 28 Apr 14

Domaine des Baumard Under Screw Cap: 10 Years After

Domaine des Baumard's Florent Baumard visits Wine Spectator senior editor James Molesworth to taste through 10 years of the Loire Valley icon's wines under screw cap.

I attended a big party in Chateaneuf du Pape several years ago where they served the white wine from Pierre Usseglio of 2004 to a large crowd. It was very interesting to see the extreme differences in color and to taste the different stages of oxidation of the various bottles that were opened. One of the more well known wine myths in the Rhone is IMO based on premox: Beaucastel Rousanne VV supposedly either should be drunk young or after 10-12 years. In the intermediate period there would be a fair chance that the wine is in an awkward oxidized state. Yeah right … I‘ve had some of those awkward Beaucastel’s and they were clearly suffering from premox. No way those dark coloured oxidized wines would have been fine if I would have opened them 10 years later. Maybe Burgundy can try to sell the same wine myth for their wines to cover up the premox problem

But it’s just nonsense to say “Wine making was horrible up to the 80’s by today’s standards.” I got seriously interested in wine in the early '80s. In that decade, I drank many fine classified growth Bordeaux from “off vintages” of the 60s and 70s that were beautiful wines. There were lots of good cru bourgeois producers, too. (There were many more very difficult vintages in those decades than since, so there certainly were lots of inferior wines.)

People used to diss traditional Barolo (too much exposure to air, leading to oxidative notes and VA) as primitive. But, damn, if a lot of those wines from the 60s and 70s, even from lesser producers, haven’t turned out to be stunning.

And, for many of us, some of the “advances” in winemaking have not been positive. In Bordeaux, later picking and the widespread use of reverse osmosis to concentrate wines has raised alcohol levels substantially and cost the wines a lot of their precision, subtlety and personality. Likewise in many other regions, excessive use of new oak has meant that you can’t tell what the grape was or what country it was from.

Chris de W.:

It is well documented that variations in air permeability of cork are huge and that this causes random oxidation of wines.

I really do not see why one would not want to see that resolved before looking at other issues that could possibly effect the overall ageing potential of wines (such as increasing SO2, of which I’m not certain that I would be happy with that for health reasons).

For the third time, I agree that corks are part of the premox problem. That seems to be the only explanation for the randomness. And, yes, TCA was a terrible problem in the 1980s, 1990s and early 2000s.

So it’s fair for you to argue in favor of some alternative to traditional corks. But what most people here are trying to discuss and understand is what made the wines of Burgundy in particular so vulnerable to cork variability when they were not before sometime in the 1990s.

William Kelley:
Chris de W.:

I attended a big party in Chateaneuf du Pape several years ago where they served the white wine from Pierre Usseglio of 2004 to a large crowd. It was very interesting to see the extreme differences in color and to taste the different stages of oxidation of the various bottles that were opened. One of the more well known wine myths in the Rhone is IMO based on premox: Beaucastel Rousanne VV supposedly either should be drunk young or after 10-12 years. In the intermediate period there would be a fair chance that the wine is in an awkward oxidized state. Yeah right … I‘ve had some of those awkward Beaucastel’s and they were clearly suffering from premox. No way those dark coloured oxidized wines would have been fine if I would have opened them 10 years later. Maybe Burgundy can try to sell the same wine myth for their wines to cover up the premox problem

Funnily enough, Jasper Morris advanced just this argument in the World of Fine Wine not long ago, contending that global warming had caused the phenomenon to move further north and citing the fact that the 1996 white Burgundies have ‘recovered’ from premox in support of this thesis…

That was a very interesting article. As I recall, Morris’s argument was directly opposed to what I think Chris is saying. (I take it Chris says the Beaucastel was oxidized.) What Morris was saying was that a lot of bottles that are perceived and labeled as pre-moxed may not be oxidized. I don’t think he was contending that wines can actually be pre-moxed and then emerge from that state.

I found that interesting because I’ve had many bottles of white (not Burgundy) that what appeared to be an oxidized note when first poured that then cleaned up with air. I started a thread on that once. As I recall, someone suggested that there are some mercaptans or other sulfur compounds that can be perceived like madeirized scents but are different.

I don’t think Morris was denying that there are too many genuinely pre-moxed Burgundies – just that there may be something else going on a well and that the two are sometimes confused.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Silicone-coated corks started being used in the mid-90s. They thwart a good seal. Whether the wines from then on in Burgundy were more fragile or the wines just couldn’t withstand the gaps in the cork neck between a rigid cork/problem seal and a parrafin coated cork-better seal is somewhat of a “chicken and egg theory”. Where the seal is good, it doesn’t seem that the wines prematurely oxidize…where they aren’t good, random bottles spoil. So, whether the cork is “part” of the issue or the catalyst for bad bottles, the change to rigid corks occurred at the same time as the premox started.

I am one who thinks the term “oxidized” in this context is not necessarily apt. I opened a 2001 Carillon Puligny- Perrieres for Thanksgiving day. It was dark and odd (so I opened a Niellon 2000 Champsgain). When I went to throw the Carillon down the drain, I tasted it again (hours later) it was not totally unpleasant as “oxidized” wines can be. So, we kept it to cook with. I’ve had bottles that some thought were oxidized that, with aeration, turned out fine. This one, though, was just odd…it didn’t morph into a beauty; it just wasn’t unpleasant to taste and wasn’t devoid of all that one looks for in a WB. Its aromatics were really flat/dead, though.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
I don’t know that there is ‘scientific’ proof that there was no premox before the mid '90s, but, as I say, I have been going to Burgundy for 40 years and nobody ever mentioned the subject to me until the 21st century. And, what is the ‘scientific’ proof that there was a bad cork rate of 10 to 15% in the '80s?? I don’t remember that. Even during the worst days of bad corks, the rate rarely exceeded 5%.

There reason nobody talks about premoxed white Rhones and Loire wines is that hardly anybody collects them, compared to the number of people who collect(ed) white Burgundy. My 2005 Huet wines went quickly, hardly lasted the decade. As Lavigne and Dubourdieu noted, Burgundy is not the only place where people bought new presses or reduced SO2 use, for that matter.

I suppose people bought new presses in the '90s because
1/new press fever hit…this is a syndrome created by press sales people…or as a friend calls it, big toys for big boys
2/the new presses make life easier
3/the old presses were really old

To say that wine is better or worse than it was in the '70s is kind of silly. There was a charm about the business that is lacking today. Off vintages of Bordeaux chateaux were bottled and sold cheaply. We tried to figure out who made really good wine at a lower price and load up on it. Now chateaux have three tiers of labels and charge a month’s salary for a case of their best. Burgundy could be a bit funky, esp the reds, but the whites were glorious…after some bottle ageing.

posts.name: John Morris

Mel Knox:

And, what is the ‘scientific’ proof that there was a bad cork rate of 10 to 15% in the '80s?? I don’t remember that. Even during the worst days of bad corks, the rate rarely exceeded 5%.

I saw many estimates of 8%-12%, and that accorded with my own experience. In a tasting group I host monthly where we taste eight wines, we averaged one corked bottle per tasting for much of the 2000s. Most of that was current releases. That would be 12.5%. The rate is much, much lower now. It’s pretty rare to have an infected bottle in that group now.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked: Wouldn’t this rate depend upon the sensitivities of those present? How do you really know what TCA rates work unless each and every wine were actually tested? Something to consider . . .

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
When I say we averaged one in eight wines corked, I mean where there was a consensus that a wine was corked. There are almost always a couple of people who don’t perceive it, and sometimes initially some people aren’t sure. But after a few minutes and repeated tastes, there’s usually a pretty clear consensus. While sensitivities vary, there seem to be a lot of people with fairly similar sensitivities.

As for the estimates, Wikipedia says:

The cork-industry group APCOR cites a study showing a 0.7-1.2% taint rate. > In a 2005 study of 2800 bottles tasted at the Wine Spectator blind-tasting facilities in Napa, California, 7% of the bottles were found to be tainted.
In 2013, the Cork Quality Council ran over 25 thousand tests. > The results, compared with data from eight years ago, show a sharp reduction in TCA levels, of around 81 percent. > In the last test, 90 percent of samples of natural cork stopper shipments showed values of under 1.0 ppt and only 7 percent had showed results of 1.0-2.0 ppt.

A reduction of 81% implies that the rates were formerly almost five times these levels. And the study cited found an average TCA level of 4 ppt in 2001, dropping to something like .75 ppt now.

Mel Knox:

And, what is the ‘scientific’ proof that there was a bad cork rate of 10 to 15% in the '80s?? I don’t remember that. Even during the worst days of bad corks, the rate rarely exceeded 5%.

Mel,

The 10-15% came from a study that I read a very long time ago; one where they actually measured TCA in in a lab instead of letting humans detect TCA. A quick google search unfortunately doesn’t show a link to this study.

The limited google search does show a lot more recent ‘studies’, from periods in which the cork industry had already made improvements to their product (and with that change introduced a potential cause for people looking for what could explain an increase in perceived premox issues).

The multi-year wine spectator ‘study’ put the number of wine bottles flawed by TCA at a high of 9.5% in 2007, however they only started measuring since 2005

Wine Spectator – 9 Jan 07

Wine Flaws: Cork Taint and TCA

You've opened a bottle of wine that's supposed to be outstanding. But when you put your nose to the glass, it smells like something you pulled out from a forgotten corner of a damp basement. What's the problem? Most likely it's TCA.

More importantly, most of the numbers mentioned are based on human detection of TCA. It is highly likely that TCA will already negatively influence the smell and taste of wine at infection levels below levels that even sensitive humans can clearly detect as TCA. See for example:

Wine Spectator – 19 Sep 13

New TCA Study Confirms Suspicions About Cork-Tainted Wine

Wine Spectator senior editor James Laube has long suspected that TCA was not just a fault in wine, but that it impeded his ability to smell.

or:

http://clospepe.com/wes-blog/2014/8/4/842014-can-natural-cork-change-25-40-of-the-wine-it-touches

If humans detected that 7-9.5% of the bottles were infected by TCA in 2005-2007 when the cork industry had already improved some of its output, the percentage of wine bottles that were influenced by TCA a decade earlier was likely significantly higher than that.

Several friends of mine oversaw QC for large wineries. Their estimate of corked bottles on wines that had been aged in stainless steel was between 3 and 5 %.

When the cork companies started to get bad press in the mid '90s, some of them pointed to --who else–the barrel companies and the wineries as un-indicted conspirators. Indeed, at least one cooperage had major jssues with TCA. Regular readers of the Spectator will recall its argument with Beaulieu Vineyards and others in this matter. As wineries, cooperages and cork companies have invested lots of money in the contamination issues, things are a lot better.

One point made by the cork companies was that if you had borderline TCA in a cork, and then there was borderline TCA in the barrel, the total added up to quite noticeable and they got the blame.

What I remember is that in the '70s and '80s, corked bottles were rare.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
This thread has made me think of the logic here, about the premox…not the TCA issues it has digressed to. If corks are the only plausible explanation for variability within given cases being “premoxed”, isn’t the cork (by that I mean the integrity of the seal, not the inherent flaws in modern corks) one plausible explanation for the whole shebang? We are only experiencing the premox in those “random” bottles…the other wines in a given case, presumably, are fine.

Maybe that’s the reason no one has figured it out…the “answer” is too rudimentary: poor seals (due to rigid, silicone-coated corks not being able to make a good seal at the bottle neck). Most people are assuming that the wines themselves are the “problems”…and that the “problems” just show up randomly due to the corks’ inability to make a good seal. Maybe, those bottles are the extent of the problem?

I’m happy to hear contrary “logic” or opinions, but…this thread has made me think about this issue in a new way. I had always assumed it was the wines’ vulnerability that was the “problem” and that the corks determine which bottles it would manifest itself in. Maybe it’s not the wines at all…it would have happened to “old style” whites too…if the cork couldn’t create a good seal. (And it did happen with Lafon wines in the '80s, as they were , IMO, more vulnerable due to their excessive batonnage and other “oxidating” practices in the cellar, resulting in a uniquely tropical character in them.)

posts.cooked:
G Williams:

I found a good article in PUNCH written by Jon Bonne about “browning” whites before fermentation.

http://punchdrink.com/articles/a-new-era-for-white-wine-burgundy/?cdi=2D1826EBA3A96115E0534FD66B0A4057%7d&ref=PRHAAC21A01F285&aid=randohouseinc22821-20&linkid=PRHAAC21A01F285

That is a good article, which makes some important points. There are a few questionable contentions: e.g. what Bonné calls ‘the Roulot technique’ of finishing elevage sur lie in tank had been practiced by the Domaine Leflaive for years before. But I think the bigger problem is that he is offering a mono-causal explanation for a multifactorial problem. Most of the things you can do wrong in white wine making end up with the wine oxidized, so there are a lot of different routes to get there. Closures are one factor, and in some cases probably a sufficient explanation of premox, but white Burgundy has aged just fine with questionable-quality corks in the past, and I would rather have a case of 1979 Lafon 1er crus with scrappy corks than a case of 2014 Lafon 1er crus under DIAM. Jon is not the first to make a persuasive case for the importance of juice browning (just one facet of a number of issues which follow from white winemaking with modern presses). Sulphur levels are another. So, surely, is the duration of sur lie aging. And so are bottling practices and the processing of the wine. And so are must acidities. The list goes on, which is why threads on premox tend to go round in circles. When I purchase white Burgundy to age I seek out producers making wines using old-fashioned, artisanal techniques and bottling with the best natural corks obtainable; because I am looking for wines which age like their predecessors from the '70s and '80s made in that way. But everybody has their own strategy and desired outcome.

This so-called browning technique was popularized in California in the early '80s by Zelma Long and Dave Ramey, then of Simi. Right after Dave got the reins at Matanzas Creek–around 1985-- I took Dominique Lafon to see him. Dave explained the theory and Dominique responded, I always wondered why the must turned the color of tea and then cleared up. In other words, that was not a new practice to Dominique.

One of my thoughts about the new presses is that this stage might be skipped, whereas the old Vaslin presses insured that it happened.

By the way, with these Spectator articles aren’t we
1/comparing TCA rates to oxygen barrier issues
2/comparing TCA and TBA in finished wine to TCA coming from the cork
??
Gosh, maybe the TCA levels stopped people from noticing the premox…

The floors of containers are made with wood that is treated with bromine. So from day one there are contamination issues that nobody knew about until about fifteen years ago. I have always wondered if the practice of coating the outsides of barrels with mildecide or linseed oil locked in the TBA. When I sold French bottles, every now and then I would get a call from my trucker, who would point out that the container smelled like the boys’ locker room in junior high. I didn t know what the problem was but I made sure the bottles got washed. With barrel containers we test them before loading or lay down a barrier between the floor and the barrels and pray.
